section.title: The Second Journey: Thessalonica, Berea, and Athens
section.sectionRationale: In three cities the gospel meets three responses. At Thessalonica Paul reasons from the Scriptures that the Christ must suffer and rise, and a mob accuses the believers of proclaiming another king, Jesus. At Berea the hearers eagerly test his message against Scripture. At Athens Paul confronts the sophisticated pagan mind head on, standing at the Areopagus to proclaim the one Creator God who commands all to repent because He has fixed a day of judgment and raised the Man who will judge.
section.thesis: The risen Christ is the crucified Messiah promised in Scripture and the Lord of every nation, so that the gospel both fulfils the Hebrew Scriptures for the synagogue and dismantles idolatry for the philosopher, summoning Jew and Greek alike to repent in light of the coming judgment guaranteed by the resurrection.
section.frameNote: Three cities: the reasoned proclamation and uproar at Thessalonica; the noble, Scripture-testing Bereans; and Athens, where Paul is provoked by the idols, debates the philosophers, and delivers the Areopagus address on the unknown God, ending with repentance, judgment, and resurrection.

verses.text: When they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue.
As was his custom, Paul went into the synagogue, and on three Sabbaths he reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
explaining and proving that the Christ had to suffer and rise from the dead. “This Jesus I am proclaiming to you is the Christ,” he declared.
Some of the Jews were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, along with a large number of God-fearing Greeks and quite a few leading women.

The Jews, however, became jealous. So they brought in some troublemakers from the marketplace, formed a mob, and sent the city into an uproar. They raided Jason’s house in search of Paul and Silas, hoping to bring them out to the people.
But when they could not find them, they dragged Jason and some other brothers before the city officials, shouting, “These men who have turned the world upside down have now come here,
and Jason has welcomed them into his home. They are all defying Caesar’s decrees, saying that there is another king, named Jesus!”
On hearing this, the crowd and city officials were greatly disturbed.
And they collected bond from Jason and the others and then released them.
As soon as night had fallen, the brothers sent Paul and Silas away to Berea. On arriving there, they went into the Jewish synagogue.
Now the Bereans were more noble-minded than the Thessalonians, for they received the message with great eagerness and examined the Scriptures every day to see if these teachings were true.
As a result, many of them believed, along with quite a few prominent Greek women and men.
But when the Jews from Thessalonica learned that Paul was also proclaiming the word of God in Berea, they went there themselves to incite and agitate the crowds.
The brothers immediately sent Paul to the coast, but Silas and Timothy remained in Berea.
Those who escorted Paul brought him to Athens and then returned with instructions for Silas and Timothy to join him as soon as possible.

units.label: Paul provoked in Athens
units.note: the city full of idols, the philosophers curious

verses.text: While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, he was deeply disturbed in his spirit to see that the city was full of idols.
So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and God-fearing Gentiles, and in the marketplace with those he met each day.

Some Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also began to debate with him. Some of them asked, “What is this babbler trying to say?” Others said, “He seems to be advocating foreign gods.” They said this because Paul was proclaiming the good news of Jesus and the resurrection.
So they took Paul and brought him to the Areopagus, where they asked him, “May we know what this new teaching is that you are presenting?
For you are bringing some strange notions to our ears, and we want to know what they mean.”
Now all the Athenians and foreigners who lived there spent their time doing nothing more than hearing and articulating new ideas.

units.label: Paul's address at the Areopagus
units.note: the unknown God now proclaimed, judgment and resurrection

verses.text: Then Paul stood up in the meeting of the Areopagus and said, “Men of Athens, I see that in every way you are very {{deisidaimon|religious}}.

For as I walked around and examined your objects of worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. Therefore what you worship as something {{agnostos|unknown}}, I now proclaim to you.
The God who made the world and everything in it is the Lord of heaven and earth and does not live in temples made by human hands.
Nor is He served by human hands, as if He needed anything, because He Himself gives everyone life and breath and everything else.
From one man He made every nation of men, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and He determined their appointed times and the boundaries of their lands.
God intended that they would seek Him and perhaps reach out for Him and find Him, though He is not far from each one of us.
‘For in Him we live and move and have our being.’ As some of your own poets have said, ‘We are His offspring.’
Therefore, being offspring of God, we should not think that the Divine Being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by man’s skill and imagination.
Although God overlooked the ignorance of earlier times, He now commands all people everywhere to {{metanoeo|repent}}.
For He has set a day when He will judge the world with justice by the Man He has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising Him from the dead.”
When they heard about the {{anastasis|resurrection}} of the dead, some began to mock him, but others said, “We want to hear you again on this topic.”
At that, Paul left the Areopagus.
But some joined him and believed, including Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others who were with them.

deisidaimon.gloss: Religious, reverent toward the gods, a word that can carry the shade of superstitious. Paul opens with a careful compliment that is also a probe: the Athenians are scrupulously devout, yet their very altars betray that they do not know the God they worship. It is a bridge to the gospel, not flattery.

agnostos.gloss: Unknown, unknowable by human effort. The root lies behind the English agnostic. The Athenians hedged their piety with an altar to an unknown god; Paul seizes on it to announce that this God has now made Himself known in the Creator revealed and in the risen Man He appointed.

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, to change one's mind and turn. It is more than regret; it is a turning of the whole person from idols to the living God. Paul says God now commands all people everywhere to repent, a universal summons grounded in the coming judgment and the resurrection of Christ.

anastasis.gloss: Resurrection, a rising again. It is the hinge of Paul's argument and the point at which the philosophers divide: God has given proof to all by raising the appointed Man from the dead. The resurrection guarantees the judgment and validates the claim that Jesus is Lord of every nation.

basic.context: Paul moves through three cities. In Thessalonica he reasons from the Scriptures that the Christ had to suffer and rise, wins some, and is chased out by a jealous mob crying that he proclaims another king, Jesus. In Berea the hearers eagerly test his message against Scripture and many believe. In Athens, provoked by a city full of idols, Paul debates the philosophers and then addresses the Areopagus about the unknown God.
basic.hermeneutics: Watch how Paul adapts his approach without changing his message. With Jews and God-fearers he argues from Scripture; with pagan philosophers who do not accept the Scriptures he begins from creation, conscience, and even their own poets, then drives to the same points, repentance, judgment, and the resurrection of Jesus. The key question the passage raises is how far a Christian may engage pagan thought while still preaching Christ crucified and risen.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls the Athenians deisidaimon, very religious, a careful bridge. He points to their altar to an agnostos, unknown, god and announces the God now made known. He ends by commanding all to metanoeo, repent, because of the coming judgment proved by the anastasis, the resurrection, the very word that splits his audience.
basic.typology: The altar to an unknown god pictures the longing of the nations, groping after a God they cannot reach. Paul presents Christ, the appointed Man, as the true meeting point of God and humanity. The scattering of the nations from one man and the fixing of their boundaries recall Genesis, now gathered under the risen Lord.
basic.christ: In every city Christ is the message: the suffering and rising Messiah of the Scriptures at Thessalonica and Berea, and the appointed Man raised from the dead at Athens. His resurrection is the proof God gives to all that He will judge the world in righteousness through Him.
basic.god: God is the Creator who needs nothing and gives everything, Lord of heaven and earth, sovereign over the nations and their times and places. He is not distant but near, and He now commands all people everywhere to repent, no longer overlooking ignorance.
basic.crossReferences: The Thessalonian letters grow from this visit. Genesis 1 and Deuteronomy 32 stand behind Paul's account of creation and the nations. Isaiah 44 mocks the folly of idols, as Paul does at Athens. Romans 1 explains the ignorance and idolatry Paul confronts.
basic.oneStory: The gospel confronts the pagan mind at the intellectual capital of the world. The one story of Scripture, the Creator reclaiming the nations through His Son, reaches the philosophers of Athens, where the risen Christ is announced as Lord of every people and the ground of the coming judgment.

context.literary: Three scenes in ascending significance, climaxing in the Areopagus speech, one of the great set-piece addresses of Acts and the fullest example of Paul preaching to pure pagans. Luke contrasts the jealous Thessalonians, the noble Bereans, and the curious Athenians as three responses to the one word.
context.historical: Thessalonica was a free city loyal to Rome, so the charge of proclaiming another king was politically explosive. Athens, though past its glory, remained the university of the ancient world, home to Epicureans, who denied divine providence, and Stoics, who taught a pantheistic reason pervading all. The Areopagus was the venerable council that oversaw religion and ideas.
context.canonical: The Areopagus address is the classic biblical model for engaging a non-Jewish, philosophical culture. It shows continuity with the reasoning from Scripture in the synagogue, yet begins from creation and conscience, and it fixes repentance, judgment, and resurrection as the non-negotiable core of the gospel to the nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the gospel advancing into the Greek world and to model faithful proclamation to both Jew and Gentile: the same Christ, crucified and risen, argued from Scripture where Scripture is granted and from creation and conscience where it is not, always calling hearers to repent before the coming Judge.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The particular speeches and outcomes are descriptive. The pattern is instructive: engage people where they are, honour whatever truth they hold, but always press toward sin, repentance, and the risen Christ. The prescriptive core is that God commands all people everywhere to repent in view of the judgment.

debates.question: How far should Paul's Areopagus method sanction engaging pagan thought, and did he compromise or succeed?

views.label: A model of wise contextual engagement
views.case: Paul quotes pagan poets, starts from the altar and from creation, and builds a bridge to the gospel, yet he never trims the offense: he proclaims one Creator, condemns idolatry, and ends with repentance, judgment, and resurrection. Some mocked, but some believed, including Dionysius and Damaris, so the sermon was fruitful, not a failure.

views.label: A caution against over-accommodation
views.case: Others note Paul's later resolve to know nothing but Christ crucified at nearby Corinth, 1 Corinthians 2:2, and read Athens as a lesson that philosophical common ground must never blunt the cross. Rightly held, this is not a rebuke of Paul but a guard: the method is sound only because it still arrives at sin, the cross implied in judgment, and the resurrection.

debates.bearing: Together the views teach that Christians may honour and use whatever is true in a culture, but must always confront idolatry and preach the crucified and risen Christ who calls all to repent; engagement that never reaches the cross has lost the gospel.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's speech is a tissue of well-chosen words. He names the Athenians deisidaimon, scrupulously religious, then turns their altar to an agnostos, unknown, god into a doorway for the God now revealed. He grants what is true in their poets, that we are God's offspring, only to expose the folly of idols. And he ends with the double hammer of metanoeo, repent, and anastasis, resurrection: God commands all to turn, because He has fixed a day of judgment and proved it by raising the appointed Man.

typology.type: The altar to an unknown god
typology.fulfillment: The blind longing of the nations for the God they cannot find by searching, answered as Paul proclaims the Creator made known in the risen Christ.

typology.type: The nations set within their boundaries, from one man
typology.fulfillment: The scattering and ordering of the peoples in Genesis, now gathered under the appointed Man whom God has raised to be Lord of every nation.

typology.type: The appointed Man who will judge the world
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man given dominion over all peoples, Daniel 7:13-14, fulfilled in Jesus, raised as the righteous Judge of all.

study.christ: Christ stands at the centre of all three scenes. To the synagogue He is the Messiah who, according to the Scriptures, had to suffer and rise; to the philosophers He is the appointed Man raised from the dead. His resurrection is not a footnote but the proof God offers to the whole world that He will judge in righteousness through His Son. To reject the resurrection, as the mockers did, is to refuse both the Judge and the Saviour.
study.god: The God Paul proclaims is the Creator of all, self-sufficient and giving life and breath to all, Lord of heaven and earth who does not dwell in man-made temples. He is sovereign over the nations, their times and borders, yet near to each person, so that they might seek Him. Once He overlooked ignorance; now, having spoken finally in the risen Christ, He commands all people everywhere to repent before the day of judgment.

crossReferences.note: The Thessalonians turned from idols to serve the living God and to wait for His Son, whom He raised, echoing this visit's message.
The prophets' mockery of idols made by human hands stands behind Paul's critique at Athens.
Paul's fuller account of idolatry, ignorance, and the suppression of the truth that he confronts in Athens.
God will judge the world by the Man He appointed and raised, tying repentance to the coming judgment guaranteed by the resurrection.

study.oneStory: At the intellectual heart of the Greek world, the gospel confronts the pagan mind. Paul honours what truth the Athenians hold, exposes the folly of their idols, and proclaims the Creator now made known in the risen Christ, calling all to repent before the coming Judge. In the sweep of Scripture this is the Creator reclaiming the scattered nations through His Son, so that even Athens hears that Jesus is Lord and some believe.

quiz.q: What made the Bereans more noble-minded than the Thessalonians?

options.text: They received the message eagerly and examined the Scriptures daily to test it.
options.note: Luke praises their eager reception joined to careful testing of the message against Scripture.

options.text: They believed Paul immediately without any hesitation.
options.note: Many did believe, but their nobility lay in eager yet testing hearing, not in uncritical acceptance.

options.text: They were more educated than the people of Thessalonica.
options.note: The text commends their attitude to Scripture, not their level of education.

options.text: They rejected the Scriptures in favour of Paul's authority.
options.note: The opposite is true: they weighed Paul's message by the Scriptures.

quiz.q: How did Paul begin his address to the Athenian philosophers?

options.text: From their altar to an unknown god and from the one Creator of all.
options.note: Paul starts with the altar and creation, common ground the pagans could grant, then moves to the gospel.

options.text: By quoting the Hebrew prophets to prove Jesus is the Messiah.
options.note: That was his method in the synagogue; with pagans who did not accept Scripture he began from creation and their own poets.

options.text: By condemning the city and refusing any common ground.
options.note: He was provoked by the idols, yet he engaged them, beginning with their altar rather than pure condemnation.

options.text: By affirming that all their gods were valid paths to truth.
options.note: Paul denied their idols; he did not endorse them.

quiz.q: On what basis does Paul say God now commands everyone to repent?

options.text: Because God has set a day of judgment, proven by raising the appointed Man from the dead.
options.note: Paul grounds the call to repent in the coming judgment guaranteed by the resurrection of Jesus.

options.text: Because idolatry is foolish and beneath rational people.
options.note: He does expose the folly of idols, but the stated ground for repentance is the judgment secured by the resurrection.

options.text: Because the Athenians were more religious than most.
options.note: He notes their religiosity as a bridge, not as the reason to repent.

options.text: Because God will overlook their ignorance forever.
options.note: Paul says God once overlooked ignorance but now commands repentance; He will not overlook it forever.

quiz.q: Why did the mob in Thessalonica accuse Paul of defying Caesar?

options.text: Because he proclaimed another king, Jesus, which sounded like treason.
options.note: The charge was that Paul preached another king, Jesus, against Caesar's decrees.

options.text: Because he had led a violent revolt in the city.
options.note: There was an uproar, but it was stirred by his opponents; the accusation centred on proclaiming Jesus as king, not on a revolt he led.

options.text: Because he refused to pay Roman taxes.
options.note: Taxes are not in view; the charge concerned proclaiming another king.

options.text: Because he denied that God exists.
options.note: Paul proclaimed the living God and the risen Christ, not atheism.

quiz.q: How did the Athenians respond to Paul's message about the resurrection?

options.text: Some mocked, some wanted to hear more, and some believed.
options.note: Luke records all three responses, including the conversion of Dionysius and Damaris.

options.text: The whole council was persuaded and believed at once.
options.note: Some did believe, but many mocked or deferred; it was not a wholesale conversion.

options.text: They arrested Paul and put him on trial for his life.
options.note: They debated and dismissed him, but there was no trial for his life here.

options.text: They all rejected him and no one believed.
options.note: Some did believe, including Dionysius the Areopagite and Damaris.

questions: The Bereans received the word eagerly yet tested everything by Scripture. How well do you hold together an open heart and a discerning mind when you hear teaching?
Paul was deeply disturbed by a city full of idols. What are the idols of your own culture, and does their grip on people move you as they moved Paul?
Paul honoured what truth the Athenians held while still calling them to repent. Where do you find it hardest to combine respect for others with faithfulness to the whole gospel?
Paul says God is not far from each one of us and commands all to repent. Is there an area where God is near and calling you to turn to Him afresh?
The resurrection was the point where Paul's hearers divided. How does the reality of the risen, appointed Judge shape the way you live and speak this week?

translationNotes.note: Very religious renders a Greek word that can also carry the sense of superstitious. Paul's opening is a careful, double-edged compliment, acknowledging their devotion while exposing its ignorance. Footnote markers in the source were omitted when building the verse text.
From one man renders literally from one; some manuscripts read from one blood. The sense is the same, that God made every nation from a single origin, underscoring the unity of the human race.

Paul cites pagan writers: for in Him we live and move and have our being is likely from Epimenides, and we are His offspring from the poem Phainomena by Aratus. Paul uses their words to press home the Creator's claim.

No verses are omitted in this chapter. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
